content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

August 24, 1987

Jan P. Stieger
California Optometric PAC
P.O. Box 2591
Sacramento, CA 95812

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-87-175

Dear Ms. Stieger:

You have requested advice concerning the campaign disclosure
provisions of the Political Reform Act.1/

QUESTION

When the California Optometric Political Action Committee
(Cal-OPAC) reports contributions received from individual members
of the California Optometric Association, is it permissible to
report only the cumulative amount received from each member who
has contributed $100 or more during the calendar year?

CONCLUSION

Cal-OPAC must report the amount received from each member
during the period covered by the campaign disclosure statement as
well as the cumulative amount received during the calendar year.

FACTS

The California Optometric Association collects member
contributions to Cal-OPAC when it collects monthly membership
dues. Monthly payments to Cal-OPAC are approximately $10 per
member, although some members make advance payments and some
members make late payments. The Association periodically
transmits a lump sum amount of member contributions to Cal-OPAC,
which reports the Association as an intermediary for those
contributions on its campaign disclosure reports. After reporting

_______________________

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Administrative Code
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Administrative Code.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Jan P. Stieger
Page 2

the lump sum amount received from the Association during the
period, Cal-OPAC lists the name and address of each member who has
met the $100 itemization threshold and provides the cumulative
amount of contributions received from that member since January 1
of the current calendar year. Recently, the Political Reform
Division of the Secretary of State's office requested an amendment
to Cal-OPAC's campaign disclosure report for the period
October 19, 1986 through December 31, 1986 disclosing the amount
received from each member during the period covered by the report
as well as the cumulative amount.

Cal-OPAC believes that providing this information will be
extremely burdensome, particularly when the amount received from
each member during a single reporting period is usually only $10
to $20 and the PAC's reports are normally over 100 pages long. In
addition, Cal-OPAC states that it will be difficult, if not
impossible, to have the information generated automatically by
computer as is the rest of the information contained in its report.

ANALYSIS

The Act requires organizations which receive $500 in a
calendar year for the purpose of making contributions to
officeholders, candidates and committees to file periodic reports
disclosing funds received and payments made. (Sections 82013,
82200-84217.) Section 84211 states that each campaign statement
must include the following information:

...If the cumulative amount of contributions (including loans)
received from a person is one hundred dollars ($100) or more
and a contribution or loan has been received from that person
during the period covered by the campaign statement:

(1)  His or her full name;

(2)  His or her street address;

(3)  His or her occupation;

(4)  The name of his or her employer or if self-employed,
name of business;

(5)  The date and amount received for each contribution
received during the period covered by the campaign
statement and if the contribution is a loan, the interest
rate for the loan; and

(6)  The cumulative amount of contributions....

(Section 84211(f). Emphasis
added.)

Jan P. Stieger
Page 3


The Fair Political Practices Commission is the agency with
primary responsibility for the impartial, effective administration
and implementation of the Act.  (Section 83111.)  Although the
Commission is aware of and is sympathetic to the burden placed on
candidates and committees required to comply with the Act's
detailed disclosure requirements, Section 84400 of the campaign
disclosure provisions specifically prohibits the Commission from
granting exemptions from the requirements:

     Notwithstanding any other provision of the law, the
     commission shall have no power to exempt any person, including
     any candidate or committee, from any of the requirements
     imposed by the provisions of this chapter.


     Therefore, Cal-OPAC is required to provide the amount of
contributions received from each member during the period covered
by each campaign disclosure report if the total amount of
contributions received from that member is $100 since January 1 of
the current calendar year.

     The following changes to the reporting format enclosed with
your letter will satisfy Cal-OPAC's disclosure requirements:

     1.     In the first space on Schedule A, disclose the total
amount of contributions received during the period through the
Association.  The lump sum amount should be listed in the
"Employer" column along with a notation that the Association acted
as an intermediary for the contributions.

     2.     Each individual member whose cumulative contributions
equal $100 or more during the year should be listed as you
currently report them, except that the "Received" column must
contain the amount each member contributed during the period
covered by the report.

     3.     In the summary section at the bottom of Schedule A,
Line 1 (Amount Received--Contributions of $100 or More) should
contain the total amount of contributions received during the
period from members whose names and addresses are itemized on
Schedule A.  Line 2 (Amount Received--Contributions of Less than
$100) should contain the total amount of contributions received
during the period from members whose contributions to date are
less than $100.

     Again, please be assured that the Commission is aware of the
burden this reporting requirement places on Cal-OPAC.  However,
the language of the Act clearly states that this information must
be disclosed.  Unless the Legislature amends this section, the
Commission has no authority to permit nondisclosure of this
information.

Jan P. Stieger
Page 4


     Please do not hesitate to contact me if you have additional
questions.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Diane M. Griffiths
                                        General Counsel

                                        [illegible]

By:  Carla Wardlow
     Political Reform Consultant

DMG:CW:kmt

July 21, 1987

Ms. Carla Wardlow
Technical Analyst
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Carla:

At your suggestion, I am requesting written advice from the Fair
Political Practices Commission regarding the attached amendment request
for Form 420, Schedule A, from the Political Reform Division of the
Secretary of State.

I have attached page one of our Schedule A, to show you our current
practice in disclosing our monetary contributions. The California Optometric Association collects Cal-OPAC dues monthly with the association
dues. On a regular basis, they give us a check for the Cal-OPAC dues
they have received. Therefore, I report this lump sum amount as my
first entry on Schedule A.

Cal-OPAC dues are approximately $125 per year, and, therefore,
members are billed about $10 monthly. Whether a member is ahead or
behind in his/her dues determines when the individual exceeds the $100
threshold.

When a member exceeds $100 in contributions, I report him/her on
Schedule A; however, I report the cumulative amount (to show he has
exceeded $100) and report nothing in the "received amount" column, as
this would duplicate reporting of the money included in the first line
of Schedule A. (This money was contributed through our intermediary,
the California Optometric Association.)

Considering the extreme length of our reports (over 100 pages),
it would be a great burden on us to amend this report, or to report our
contributions in an alternate manner. According to our business manager, our computer is not capable of providing the information differently.
I request the FPPC review our case and grant permission to Cal-OPAC to
continue reporting our monetary contributions as we have for several
years.

Warmest regards,

Jan P Stieger

Jan P. Stieger, Coordinator
Political Activities

March Fong Eu

Sacramento, California 95807

POLITICAL REFORM DIVISION
(916) 322-4580

July 9, 1987

Michael Larkin, O.D., Treasurer
California Optometric Political
Action Committee aka Cal-OPAC
Identification Number 745825
3502 Katella
Los Alamitos, CA 90702

Dear Dr. Larkin:

Thank you for filing your Form 420, executed on January 30, 1987, for the
reporting period of October 19, 1986 through December 31, 1986. As required by
the Political Reform Act of 1974, we have reviewed your statement to determine
whether it conforms on its face with the disclosure requirements.

We have noted the following to assist you in complying with the requirements of
the law.

Verification

Your statement did not bear the signature of the treasurer. The law
requires that the statement bear this signature.

All campaign statements for recipient committees, including all Statements
of Organization, must be verified by the individual listed as the treasurer
on the recipient committee's Statement of Organization. If you have any
technical or legal questions regarding this matter, please contact the Fair
Political Practices Commission at (916) 322-5662.

Schedule A-Monetary Contributions

Both the received amount and cumulative to date amount must be entered for
all entries listed on Schedule A.

Enclosed is an Amendment to Campaign Disclosure Statement (Form 405). Form
405 must be completed and filed to reflect any changes to your original campaign
statement. Copies of your amendment should be filed with all offices that
received copies of the statement originally filed. Please reply within fifteen
days to this request.

If you have any questions, please call David Hulse at (916) 322-0392.

Sincerely,

BOB STEELE, Manager
Political Reform Division

Enclosure

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 28, 1987

Jan P. Stieger
Coordinator, Political Activities
California Optometric PAC
P.O. Bx 2591
Sacramento, CA  95812

Re:  87-175

Dear Ms. Stieger:

Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform
Act was received on July 24, 1987 by the Fair Political
Practices Commission.  If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact me directly at (916) 322-5662.

We try to answer all advice requests promptly.  Therefore,
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions
or more information is needed, you should expect a response
within 21 working days if your request seeks formal written
advice.  If more information is needed, the person assigned to
prepare a response to your request will contact you shortly to
advise you as to the information needed.  If your request is
for informal assistance, we will answer it as quickly as we
can.  (See Commission Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Adm. Code sec.
18329).)

You also should be aware that your letter and our response
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

Very truly yours,

Jeanne Pritchard
Chief
Technical Assistance and Analysis
Division

well as the cumulative amount received during the calendar year.
